Phase 4 — Inter-Individual Variability (IIV)

Adding random effects on CL, V, and Ka

Author

Pramod BR

Published

June 28, 2026

1 Objectives

The base model (Phase 3) allowed only clearance to vary between subjects. Here we add between-subject random effects on V and Ka as well, so each subject has their own clearance, volume, and absorption rate. We then test whether the extra variability is justified — better fit, well-estimated, and plausibly distributed.

2 What a random effect is

For each parameter we write, e.g., cl = exp(tcl + eta.cl), where eta.cl is a subject-specific deviation drawn from a normal distribution N(0, omega^2). The population shares one typical curve; each subject’s curve is a shifted version of it. The spread of those shifts — the IIV — is summarised as a CV%.

The fitted model’s structure is shown as equations in the next section.

3 Fit the IIV model

Code 
dat      <- build_nonmem_data()
base_fit <- readRDS(fs::path(PATH_MODELS, "base_fit.rds"))
iiv_fit  <- fit_iiv_model(dat)

\[\begin{align*} {ka} & = \exp\left({tka}+{eta.ka}\right) \\ {cl} & = \exp\left({tcl}+{eta.cl}\right) \\ {v} & = \exp\left({tv}+{eta.v}\right) \\ \frac{d \: depot}{dt} & = -{ka} {\times} {depot} \\ \frac{d \: center}{dt} & = {ka} {\times} {depot}-\left(\frac{{cl}}{{v}}\right) {\times} {center} \\ {cp} & = \frac{{center}}{{v}} \\ {cp} & \sim add({add.sd})+prop({prop.sd}) \end{align*}\]

4 Parameter estimates

Code 
base_param_table(iiv_fit) |>
  kbl(caption = "IIV model estimates (fixed effects, IIV CV%, and shrinkage)",
      digits = 3) |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"),
                full_width = FALSE)
IIV model estimates (fixed effects, IIV CV%, and shrinkage)
Parameter Est. SE %RSE Back-transformed(95%CI) BSV(CV%) Shrink(SD)%
tka 0.389 0.198 50.9 1.48 (1, 2.17) 68.8 1.88%<
tcl 1.03 0.074 7.22 2.79 (2.41, 3.22) 25.7 1.12%<
tv 3.47 0.046 1.33 32 (29.3, 35) 12.6 15.7%<
add.sd 0.261 0.261
prop.sd 0.136 0.136

Read the BSV(CV%) column for the size of each random effect and the Shrink(SD)% column for how well the data support it. As a rule of thumb, shrinkage under ~30% means the individual estimates are genuinely informed by the data rather than collapsing to the population mean.

5 Is the added variability justified?

We compare the base and IIV models by objective function value (OFV), and by AIC/BIC, which penalise extra parameters. By the likelihood-ratio test, adding two random effects (V and Ka) is significant at p < 0.05 if the OFV drops by more than 5.99 (χ², 2 df).

Code 
cmp <- compare_models(
  model_metrics(base_fit, "Base (IIV on CL)"),
  model_metrics(iiv_fit,  "IIV on CL, V, Ka")
)
cmp |>
  kbl(caption = "Base vs IIV model comparison", digits = 2) |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"),
                full_width = FALSE)
Base vs IIV model comparison
Model OFV AIC BIC nPar dOFV dAIC
Base (IIV on CL) 177.81 432.41 449.70 6 0.00 0.00
IIV on CL, V, Ka 104.36 362.96 386.02 8 -73.45 -69.45

The IIV model lowers the OFV by 73.45 points relative to base (ΔAIC = -69.45). A drop well beyond the 5.99 threshold means the between-subject variability on V and Ka is statistically warranted — the model genuinely fits better, not just by added flexibility.

6 Random-effect distributions

The empirical-Bayes η estimates should be centred on 0 and roughly symmetric — the model’s normality assumption.

Code 
iiv_plot_eta(iiv_fit)

7 Key takeaways

  • Random effects on V and Ka were added to the base (CL-only) model.
  • The OFV fell by 73.45 (≫ the 5.99 cut-off for 2 df), so the extra IIV is justified.
  • Shrinkage and the η distributions indicate the random effects are data-supported, not artefacts.
  • This full-IIV model is now the reference onto which we test a covariate (body weight) next.

Next: Phase 5 — Covariate model, testing whether body weight explains part of the between-subject variability in CL and V. ```
